title_en: I Remember America Before the Measles Vaccine
title_zh: 我还记得美国在麻疹疫苗出现之前的样子
rubric_en: And I wish Robert F. Kennedy Jr. did too.
rubric_zh: 而我也希望罗伯特·F·肯尼迪二世当年也能记得这点。

paragraphs.en: Lately, I’ve come to notice that the strangest and most terrible pieces of my childhood are roaring back. I was born in 1933, and much of what I remember as a little girl was defined by either the war or what we called, simply, sickness.
paragraphs.zh: 最近，我越来越觉得自己童年里最怪异、最可怕的记忆正在重现。我出生于1933年，而我作为一个小女孩时记得的很多东西，要么与战争有关，要么就是我们那时简单称作“生病”。

paragraphs.en: I myself was blessed with exceptionally good health, but my friends, family, and community were regularly struck with childhood diseases. Neighborhoods were frozen in fear when maladies suddenly erupted: pool closures during polio epidemics, quarantines when mumps or measles raged. I remember one particularly galling time when my older sister Mimi and I were confined to the house, morosely watching our friends playing on the construction site of a new house across the street. We were fine; they all had whooping cough. Whooping cough was often deadly for babies and toddlers but among the less debilitating of childhood diseases past for older children, thus the freedom to play while coughing. Neither Mimi nor I ever caught it—a fact I was grateful for 40 years later, when I met with a pulmonologist about my cigarette-compromised lungs and he remarked, “At least you never had whooping cough.”
paragraphs.zh: 我自己天生就很少生病，但我的朋友、家人和社区却常常被童年疾病频频袭击。每当疾病突然爆发，街坊邻里都会陷入恐慌：小儿麻痹症暴发时游泳池被关闭，得流行性腮腺炎或麻疹时就会实施隔离。我记得有一次让我非常难受的经历：我和姐姐米米被禁足在家，郁郁寡欢地看着朋友们在街对面一座新房的工地上玩耍。我们自己没事；他们都得了百日咳。百日咳对婴儿和幼儿常常是致命的，但在年纪稍大的孩子中，它反而算是比较不那么衰弱身心的一类童年病，所以才有人边咳嗽边自由玩耍。我和米米都没有感染——这件事在40年后让我心怀感激：我去看肺科医生，谈到我受烟草损害的肺时，他说过一句“至少你从没得过百日咳。”

paragraphs.en: We did, however, catch chicken pox simultaneously with our older sisters, Jane and Helen; we were then 5, 7, 11, and 13. Just thinking of it can resurrect the itch. (And lest I forget, some 70 years later, following a time of extended stress, that long-dormant varicella-zoster virus returned as a bout of shingles.) But that was nothing compared with the measles Jane contracted. Memories of those days, among the most vivid of my early life, still evoke tremors in the bottom of my stomach. There was widespread fear of measles causing blindness, which had indeed happened to a young family acquaintance. So for several days at the height of her illness, Jane was quarantined in one bedroom while Helen moved in with Mimi and me. The shades were drawn and curtains closed in Jane’s room, and the door was opened only after the hallway was darkened. She survived—and later went on to become a wife, mother, and well-regarded artist. But that was just the luck of the draw. Measles killed some 10,000 American children in the 1930s and ’40s—roughly 500 kids died every year. In my generation, we were the guinea pigs for what science would soon discover: This pesky childhood sickness increases the risk of stroke, chronic lung problems, and impaired neurodevelopment.
paragraphs.zh: 不过，我们确实和姐姐琼和海伦同时得了水痘；那时我们的年龄是5岁、7岁、11岁和13岁。光是想到当时的情景，我都能想起那种发痒感。（我也不想忘了：大约70年后，在一段长期高压之后，那种长期潜伏的水痘-带状疱疹病毒又卷土重来，变成了一次带状疱疹发作。）但这与琼后来得麻疹的那次相比，算不了什么。那段往事是我早年记忆中最生动的之一，至今仍会在我心底掀起一阵发抖。那时候，大家普遍担心麻疹会致盲，而这种情况确实曾发生在一位年轻的熟人家庭成员身上。所以在琼病情最重的几天里，她被隔离在一间卧室里，海伦则搬去和米米、我同住。琼的房间拉上了百叶窗，窗帘紧闭，只有走廊熄了灯之后才会开门。她活了下来，后来成为了妻子、母亲，也是一位备受尊敬的艺术家。但那只是侥幸而已。麻疹在1930年代和1940年代每年在美国夺去约1万名儿童的生命——大约每年500个孩子。到了我们这一代人，我们正像小白鼠一样，承受着医学很快就要发现的真相：这种讨厌的童年疾病会提高中风、慢性肺部问题和神经发育受损的风险。

paragraphs.en: Read: His daughter was America’s first measles death in a decade
paragraphs.zh: 相关阅读：他的女儿成为十年来美国首例麻疹死亡者

paragraphs.en: Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. was not born yet when all of this took place. By the time he turned 13, in 1967, most of the diseases that ravaged my childhood had been eradicated by the vaccines he now disdains. The unfortunate thing about that disdain is that Kennedy has the power to impose his bizarre notions on the entire country. It’s too bad that we have no way to time-capsule him back several decades (or time-travel forward, for that matter) in hopes that he might understand the havoc he will wreak upon future generations.
paragraphs.zh: 卫生部长罗伯特·F·肯尼迪 Jr. 在这一切发生时还没有出生。到他1967年13岁时，我童年时期那场场肆虐的疾病大多已被如今他鄙视的疫苗所消灭。遗憾的是，令人不幸的是，肯尼迪有能力把他那些怪异的观点强加给整个国家。可惜我们没法把他放进时间胶囊送回几十年前（或者说把他“时光穿越”到未来），以希望他能理解自己将对后代造成的破坏。

paragraphs.en: RFK Jr. would have liked my friend Jack, a rambunctious child given to sudden mischief. Jack was part of a foursome, the others being Mary Sue and Tommy and me. We bonded days after I arrived in Ashland, Virginia, having just turned 6. For several years we were inseparable, even when Jack developed rheumatic fever and was bedridden for weeks. We simply detoured from climbing trees and playing ball into spending afternoons staging battles with toy soldiers on his bed or listening, enraptured, to his favorite radio serials, including The Lone Ranger and Jack Armstrong, the All-American Boy. Jack was isolated even from the three of us when whooping cough rampaged through the town, but he still managed to catch that too. He died of heart failure at age 19; how much of that good young heart’s failure was due to those earlier illnesses, we’ll never know. That was more than half a century ago. I never forgot Jack. I wish I could tell Kennedy about him, and the pain his death caused everyone who loved him.
paragraphs.zh: RFK Jr. 若认识我的朋友杰克，一定会喜欢他。杰克是个活泼淘气、常常突然惹祸的孩子。我们四个是一个小圈子，其他三个是玛丽·苏、汤米和我。我刚满6岁到达弗吉尼亚州阿什兰时，我们很快就成了朋友。之后好些年，我们形影不离，即使杰克得了风湿热并卧床数周也是如此。我们只好放弃爬树和踢球，把下午改为在他的病床上摆弄玩具兵扮演战斗，或者专心听他最喜欢的广播连载，包括《孤星枪手》和《全美男孩杰克·阿姆斯特朗》。当百日咳在镇上横扫流行时，杰克甚至和我们三个人都被隔离了，但他仍然传染上了。杰克19岁时死于心力衰竭；关于那颗年轻的、健康的心脏有多少问题是由这些早年的疾病造成的，我们永远无法知道。那是五十多年以前的事了。我从未忘记杰克。我希望能把他和他去世给每一个爱他的人带来的痛苦告诉肯尼迪。

paragraphs.en: From the January 2026 issue: Why is Robert F. Kennedy Jr. so convinced he’s right?
paragraphs.zh: 来自2026年1月刊：为什么罗伯特·F·肯尼迪 Jr. 如此坚定地确信自己是对的？

paragraphs.en: The other childhood friend I would most like our health secretary to know is Susan, who moved to our neighborhood in second grade and contracted polio when we were in our early teens. I remember being taken to visit her when she was in an iron lung. Though she was in a highly restricted part of the hospital, I was allowed to visit, largely because she was not expected to live and we were desperate to see each other. In those days of family doctors who made house calls for everything but major emergencies, I had been in a hospital once or twice at most. I knew all about the iron lung and was thoroughly familiar with Susan’s precarious state; still, I was not prepared for the sight of a giant monster of a machine on sturdy legs, with only my friend’s head protruding from one end.
paragraphs.zh: 我最希望这位卫生部长认识的另一位童年好友是苏珊。她在我们读二年级时搬到我们邻里，我们十几岁初期时得了小儿麻痹。那时我还记得自己曾去探望她，当时她正在使用“铁肺”。尽管她在医院里一个管控最严格的区域里，我还是被允许去看望她，主要是因为她被预估活不了多久，而我们都急切地想见彼此。当时的家庭医生会为家庭处理几乎所有小病（除重大急症），我最多也只进过一两次医院。我对铁肺很了解，也对苏珊那危险的状态非常熟悉；然而，仍没想到那台长着粗壮支柱、像头巨大怪物一般的机器，会让我看到的只是朋友的头从一端探出。

paragraphs.en: There were six of them in all, I think, in a cold room smelling of ether and rubbing alcohol: six futuristic creatures with human heads. Nurses in starched white uniforms and rubber-soled white shoes walked wordlessly among the machines, which kept up a steady thrum as they forced air in and out of failing lungs. Susan’s mother stood on one side, stroking her daughter’s hair, while Susan and I talked in voices just above a whisper, as if we were in church. She wanted to tell me about the boy who had been in the iron lung behind where I sat, who was there when she arrived but a few days ago had vanished. There was only one other visitor, another mother stroking another small head. Happy as I was to see Susan, I couldn’t help wondering if I would be able to summon the courage to endure such hardship just to survive. But survive she did, unexpectedly, to live to adulthood with some disabilities.
paragraphs.zh: 我想那儿一共只有六个人，全都躺在一间散发着乙醚和酒精气味的冰冷房间里：六个长着人头的未来感怪物。穿着挺括白色制服、白色胶底鞋的护士们无声地在机器间来回走动，机器持续发出稳定的嗡鸣，不停地给衰弱的肺部输送和抽出空气。苏珊的母亲站在一边，轻轻梳着女儿的头发，而我和苏珊用几乎只有耳语般的声音交谈，好像我们在教堂里。她想跟我聊一位坐在我旁边那台铁肺后面的小男孩——那个在她几天前到来时就在那里，却在最近一下子消失了。除了另一位正在抚摸另一个小小脑袋的母亲外，没有其他访客。虽然见到苏珊让我很开心，但我还是忍不住想，自己是否真能鼓起勇气，忍受如此困苦只为活下来。结果她却出乎意料地活了下来，活到了成年，虽然带着一些残疾。

paragraphs.en: From the February 1957 issue: How good is the polio vaccine?
paragraphs.zh: 出自1957年2月刊：小儿麻痹症疫苗究竟有多好？

paragraphs.en: The disabilities resulting from those childhood diseases far exceeded the recorded life-and-death statistics: the compromised lungs, the weakened hearts, the bones and muscles and systems unable to develop as they might have. It’s impossible to calculate the awful toll. Vaccines, though, changed it all, essentially vanquishing those diseases in the United States and much of the rest of the world. The rejection of science is sending us back to those dark ages.
paragraphs.zh: 那些童年疾病留下的后遗症，远远超过了官方记载的生死统计：受损的肺、衰弱的心脏、未能正常发育的骨骼、肌肉和器官系统。那些可怕的代价几乎无法计算。然而，疫苗改变了一切，基本上在美国以及世界上大部分地区根除了这些疾病。对科学的排斥正把我们带回那些黑暗时代。

paragraphs.en: When I was 12, Americans everywhere threw what can only be described as a two-day party. It was 1945, and Japan had surrendered. Euphoria swept across the country, including in small towns like Ashland, where my friends and I had pulled red wagons around to gather scrap for the war effort. There had been a slight exhaling of breath the previous May, on what came to be known as V-E Day, and another one after the bombs were dropped on Hiroshima and Nagasaki in August. (Only later would I learn the grim moral complexity of such weapons.) But with the end of the war came a widespread belief that lasting peace was no longer just a dream. Flags went up on every front porch, the sounds of long-hoarded firecrackers pierced the air, perfect strangers hugged each other on sidewalks, and high-school bands paraded in the streets.
paragraphs.zh: 我12岁那年，全美各地的人都举行了一场可以说是持续两天的狂欢。那是1945年，日本投降了。欢欣与狂喜席卷全国，也包括我在的阿什兰小镇，在那里，我和朋友们曾推着红色手推车收集废料来支援战争。前几个月，也就是之后被称为“欧洲胜利日”的五月，我国民都像长舒了一口气；而八月广岛、长崎被投下原子弹后，又是一场类似的松气（直到后来我才逐渐明白这种武器背后沉重而复杂的道德问题）。但随着战争结束，人们普遍相信持久和平不再只是梦想。每个门廊都挂起了旗帜，长年积压的鞭炮声尖利地划破空气，陌生人在人行道上拥抱，高中乐队在街头行进。

paragraphs.en: Tom Nichols: Reclaiming real American patriotism
paragraphs.zh: 汤姆·尼科尔斯：重拾真正的美国爱国主义

paragraphs.en: Those of us who are now in our 90s might be forgiven a twinge of nostalgia for that moment. But this is no plea to return to some imaginary good old days. Indelibly etched into my brain are memories from the decade leading up to our entry into the war. I was 4, at most, the night my father woke Mimi and me in what seemed the middle of the night and gently carried us downstairs into the living room. He deposited us on the floor in front of the Philco radio. We sat at the feet of our mother, who was on the sofa darning socks. There were crackling sounds coming from the radio, someone speaking over the noise of a crowd. My father explained that we were in no danger but that terrible things were happening in the world, largely because of one very bad man, and he wanted us to hear what this madman sounded like: Adolf Hitler on a shortwave-radio broadcast. We, of course, had no idea what Hitler was saying. But the angry shouts to a cheering crowd, sounds reinforced later in newsreel clips shown at movies we occasionally attended, carried a powerful message I have never forgotten. They were the sounds of evil, the antithesis of “Love thy neighbor.”
paragraphs.zh: 现在已近90岁的我们，也许会对那一刻怀有一丝怀旧之情。但这并不是在呼吁回到某种想象中的美好旧时光。我的脑海里铭刻着一系列深刻记忆，来自我们走入战争前的十年。那时我最多只有4岁。那天夜里——至少看起来像半夜——我父亲把米米和我轻轻叫醒，抱着我们走下楼到客厅。我们被放在菲尔科收音机前的地板上，坐在坐着补袜子的母亲脚边。广播里有沙沙的杂音，有人在高声穿越人群噪音发言。父亲解释说，我们并不危险，但世界上正在发生可怕的事，主要是因为一个非常恶劣的人，他想让我们听听这个狂人的声音：阿道夫·希特勒在短波广播中的讲话。我们当然不知道希特勒在说什么，但人群中那些激烈的嘶喊、后来在我们偶尔去看的电影新闻短片中反复出现的画面，都传递出一种我永远忘不掉的信息。那是邪恶的声音，是真正与“爱你的邻居”背道而驰的反面。

paragraphs.en: Americans survived those years on kindness and collective effort. In the 1930s, when hunger, poverty, and despair were at levels hard to imagine today, you could have nothing and still be kind. As a child who never went hungry, I was spared the traumas suffered by many, but I witnessed hardship in the nation’s psyche. My father had a job that paid enough to feed four daughters and cover the mortgage on our tiny three-bedroom house, albeit just barely. Several times a week, men in worn coats and brown fedoras in search of food and work would knock on our back door. My mother would make peanut-butter-and-jelly sandwiches, hand them to me with glasses of milk, and instruct me to be very polite to “our visitors.”
paragraphs.zh: 美国人依靠善意和集体努力挺过了那些年。1930年代，饥饿、贫困和绝望达到今天难以想象的程度时，你可能一无所有，却依然可以善良。作为一个从未挨过饿的孩子，我逃过了许多人遭受的许多创伤，但我看到了整个国家精神上的艰难。我的父亲有份工作，虽然勉强，但足够养活我们四个女儿并支付我们那座狭小三居室房子的房贷。每周有好几天，穿着旧外套、戴着棕色宽边软呢帽、寻找食物和工作的男人会敲我们后门。母亲会给我做花生酱果酱三明治，递给我一杯牛奶，并叮嘱我对“我们的来访者”一定要很有礼貌。

paragraphs.en: From the November 2025 issue: I don’t want to stop believing in America’s decency
paragraphs.zh: 摘自2025年11月号：我不想停止相信美国人的正直

paragraphs.en: Throughout World War II, we knitted socks for soldiers and went with our mother to deliver hot cross buns to neighbors when a new Gold Star was hung in someone’s front window. We kids were also serious about collecting scrap and were occasionally enlisted to help watch the skies from a small rural hut for the rare passing airplane, whose description we would carefully record in a government logbook. My memories of these long-ago years are spotty; I was just a child. Far more clearly I recall the aftermath, when all of those men (and a few women) in uniform came home—Jane married one of them—and war stories were left behind. Everyone was in a hurry to move forward into a newly peaceful world, a world without the tragedies of war abroad and the curse of sickness at home.
paragraphs.zh: 在整个二战期间，我们给士兵织袜子，并且每当有人家的窗前挂起新的金星牌（Gold Star）时，就和妈妈一起给邻居送热十字包（hot cross buns）。我们孩子们也很认真地捡拾废旧物资，还时不时被征召去一个偏僻的乡村小屋里放哨，等待偶尔掠过的飞机，然后把它的机型和特征仔细记进政府的日志本。对那些远去的岁月，我的记忆很零散；毕竟我当时还只是个孩子。更清楚地留在脑海里的，是战后余波：那些穿着军装的男人（和少数女人）全部陆续回家——简妮嫁给了他们中的一个——战争故事也随之落下帷幕。每个人都急着向前走，迈入一个新生的和平世界，一个没有海外战争悲剧、没有家门口疾病诅咒的世界。

paragraphs.en: It was a time of singular, optimistic patriotism. No one thought the road ahead would be easy; everyone believed that peace and shared prosperity were possible. For nearly a century, I’ve been privileged to watch the fits, starts, and swings of that optimism: the forward leaps of science and technology, the backward falls into tragic wars, the sidesteps into misguided ideologies. But the collective effort behind those hot cross buns and front-porch flags? That is still who we are, if we choose to be.
paragraphs.zh: 那是一个独特而充满乐观的爱国时代。没人认为眼前的路会轻松；但每个人都相信和平与共同富足是可以实现的。将近一个世纪以来，我有幸见证了这种乐观主义的抽搐、起伏和转折：科学与技术的跃进，悲惨战争中的倒退，以及对错误意识形态的绕道。可是，那种在热十字包和前门国旗背后汇聚的集体努力呢？那依然是我们是谁，只要我们愿意成为那样的人。

paragraphs.en: This article appears in the June 2026 print edition with the headline “The America I’ve Known.”
paragraphs.zh: 这篇文章出现在2026年6月的纸质版，标题为《我所认识的美国》（The America I’ve Known）。
